macro_segments.title: Introduction and Mishnah: The Husband's Pre-Annulment of Future Vows

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition from prior daf's discussion of groom and yavam
Mishnah: Husband's statement affirming future vows (Kol hanedarim she-tadori mikan v'ad she-avo)
Explanation: Difference between affirming (mekayem) and annulling (meifer) vows
Rabbi Eliezer's kal va-chomer argument for pre-annulment
Chachamim's scriptural objection: linking kiyum and hafarah
Discussion of annulment without hearing and splitting the act from effect

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Conceptual Question: Does Pre-Annulment Prevent or Annul?

micro_segments.title: Iba'ya lehu: Two competing interpretations of Rabbi Eliezer's position
First possibility: vow takes effect instantly and simultaneously becomes annulled
Second possibility: pre-annulment prevents the vow from ever taking effect
Practical difference (nafka mina): the case of someone else transferring status via ve-ani

macro_segments.title: The Ve-ani Case and the Distinction Between Hafarah and Hatarah

micro_segments.title: Gemara's example: one person takes a vow, another says ve-ani, then husband annuls
Question raised by Michael: what if the first person went to a chacham for hatarah?
Ran's distinction: hatarah is oker ha-neder me-ikaro (retroactive), hafarah is mikan u-leha'ba (prospective)
Practical consequence: with hafarah, the second person who said ve-ani remains bound
Citation of parallel Gemara in Nazir about ba'al's hafarah as migaz gayiz vs. chacham's as oker me-ikara
Ran's second question: when husband annuls after ve-ani is said, how do we know ve-ani refers to prior status?
Ran's answer: citing meat of shelamim case to show ve-ani refers to status as it existed

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis of Rabbi Eliezer's Language in the Mishnah

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's statement: if husband can annul after, shouldn't he prevent before?
Gemara's correction: shelo ba'u means vows that have yet to exist, not vows that will never exist
Ran's reinterpretation of Rabbi Eliezer's kal va-chomer: vow comes into existence already challenged
Gemara's analogy: just as one can annul one's own vows ahead of time via tenai, one should annul wife's vows ahead of time
Gemara's distinction: for one's own vows, tenai operates via taus mechanism; for wife's vows, it's hafarah mechanism
Ran's brilliant reframing: kal va-chomer compares what works after (in wife's vows hafarah works after), so it should work before

macro_segments.title: Chachamim's First Counter-Argument: The Mikveh Analogy

micro_segments.title: Chachamim's response: Mikveh analogy shows what works after doesn't necessarily work before
Explanation: Mikveh removes tumah but doesn't prevent tumah from attaching
Second part of analogy: person cannot make tumei things tahor but can prevent tahor from becoming tumei
Ran's explanation: Chachamim show that logic of kal va-chomer is context-dependent and not ironclad

macro_segments.title: Chachamim's Second and Third Counter-Arguments: Pre-dunking and Seeds

micro_segments.title: Second counter: Can you pre-dunk a vessel to prevent it from becoming tamei later?
This suggests vow does take effect and is then annulled (haylan interpretation)
Gemara's note: two analogies suggest different interpretations of Rabbi Eliezer

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Conclusion and Integration with Scriptural Argument

micro_segments.title: Rabbanan's approach: they present kal va-chomer arguments to counter either possible position

macro_segments.title: Chachamim's Second and Third Counter-Arguments: Pre-dunking and Seeds (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's response: impure seeds become tahor when planted; planted seeds cannot become tamei

macro_segments.title: Chachamim's Second and Third Counter-Arguments: Pre-dunking and Seeds (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: This response suggests lo haylan interpretation (prevents effect entirely)

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Conclusion and Integration with Scriptural Argument (continued)

micro_segments.title: Seeds example suggests lo haylan and gives support to kal va-chomer interpretation
Gemara's observation: Rabbanan themselves don't know Rabbi Eliezer's position
Gemara's final note: Chachamim invoke scriptural gezerat hakatuv over kal va-chomer
Ran's resolution: Rabbanan do use kal va-chomer in principle but scripture can override
Ran's integrated reading: earlier counter-arguments show kal va-chomer isn't ironclad; scripture settles the issue

topical_tags.term: Kol hanedarim she-tadori (all vows you make)
Ishah yekimenu ve-ishah yeferennu (wife establishes, wife annuls - scriptural verse)
